Imports¶

In [1]:
import shap
import IPython
import os
import sys
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from hashlib import sha1
import math

from sklearn.dummy import DummyRegressor
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor, plot_tree
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, GradientBoostingRegressor
from sklearn.svm import SVR

from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline, make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OrdinalEncoder, FunctionTransformer, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin

from sklearn.model_selection import (
    GridSearchCV,
    cross_val_score,
    cross_validate,
    train_test_split,
    KFold,
)

from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score, make_scorer, root_mean_squared_error

Dataset and Initial analysis¶

We have choose to do a machine learning regression analysis on dataset of New York City Airbnb listings from 2019. We will try to predict reviews_per_month, as a proxy for the popularity of the listing.

We are interested in conducting a ML experiment on real-world data which a company could potentially use as insight for their operations. Using regression may provide a little more insight rather than classification.

Our initial thoughts on the dataset are: (1) There may be some redundancy in the features (specifically in regard to the neighborhood area, neighborhood location, longitude and latitude) (2) host_name we feel is not useful but host_id could be used to see if hosts has any affect on popularity. (4) last_review is a date feature and will likely need to be transformed into a new feature like days_since_last_review. (5) Most of the numeric features seem useful and will just need to be standardized. (6) Finally we think ordinally encoding the room_type feature may provide benifical insights.

In [2]:
PATH = "data/AB_NYC_2019.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(PATH)

2. Data splitting¶

In [3]:
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(df, test_size=0.3, random_state=123)

3. EDA¶

Before any feature engineering or selection, we have chosen to drop the following features: id, host_name and host_id as they are not relevant to the model. (Note: host_id may provide interesting details if engineered properly (example: number_of_reviews/host_id), but we chose not to pursue that route).

From Table 1 we can see that the features with missing values are name, last_review and reviews_per_month. Additonally, we found that every time number_of_reviews was 0, reviews_per_month was NaN. Indicating that reviews_per_month should be 0 in these cases. We did this imputation before visualizing the data any further.

Table 2 shows that for all numeric features, except longitude and latitude, the standard deviation is relatively high in comparison to the mean (standard deviation is at least equal to the mean), indicating there are many outliers. This is further supported by the relatively high max values of each feature (compared to its respective mean), indicating right-skewed data (Table 2).

This is also seen in the box plots in Figure 1: showing that 'price', 'minimum_nights', 'number_of_reviews', 'reviews_per_month' and 'calculated_host_listings_count' have significant positive outliers. Longitude and latitude had outliers in both directions, while availability_365 had neither.

Figure 2 shows the distribution of the numerical features. All except the longitude and latitude are heavily right-skewed. Figure 3 shows the distribution of the numerical features with upper outliers after the outliers are removed. Figure 3 shows an improved distribution, but it is still right-skewed and thus even after outlier removal, log-transformation will still be necessary.

Finally, Figure 4 shows the reviews_per_month for the categorical features. The figure shows that there is no significant difference between the different categories of each feature in relation to reviews_per_month and thus indicates ordinal encoding should not be used.

We will use RMSE to evaluate the average prediction error in the same units as the target. We may also use R-squared to assess how much of the variance in the target is explained by the model. This combination provides both absolute and relative performance insight.

In [4]:
BOLD = '\033[1m'
END = '\033[0m'

numerical_features = [
    'price', 
    'minimum_nights', 
    'number_of_reviews', 
    'reviews_per_month', 
    'calculated_host_listings_count', 
    'availability_365',
    'latitude',
    'longitude']

features_with_upper_outliers = ['price', 
                'minimum_nights', 
                'number_of_reviews', 
                'calculated_host_listings_count', 
                'reviews_per_month']

numerical_features_to_log = [
    'price', 
    'minimum_nights', 
    'number_of_reviews', 
    'reviews_per_month', 
    'calculated_host_listings_count', 
    'availability_365']

categorical_features = ['neighbourhood_group', 'neighbourhood', 'room_type']


# Generative AI (chatGPT) used to turn the code for a figure into a reusable function.
# Prompt "From this code [code for the plot given] create a reusable function"
def plot_feature_distributions(df, feature_list, title, n_cols=2, bins=30):
    n_features = len(feature_list)
    n_rows = math.ceil(n_features / n_cols)  # automatically compute rows

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(7*n_cols, 4*n_rows))
    axes = axes.flatten()  # flatten in case of multi-row/column grid

    for i, col in enumerate(feature_list):
        sns.histplot(data=df, x=col, bins=bins, kde=True, ax=axes[i])
        axes[i].set_title(f'Distribution of {col.capitalize()}', fontsize=14)
        axes[i].set_xlabel(col.capitalize())
        axes[i].set_ylabel('Frequency')

    # Turn off any unused subplots
    for j in range(i+1, len(axes)):
        axes[j].set_visible(False)

    # Figure title
    plt.figtext(0.5, 0.01, title, ha='center', va='center',
                fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
    plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.05, 1, 0.95])
    plt.show()


# Generative AI (chatGPT) used to help create this function.
# Prompt "Create a function to remove upper outliers of features using IQR"
def remove_upper_outliers_iqr(df, cols, k=1.5, verbose=True):
    filter_total = pd.Series(True, index=df.index)

    for col in cols:
        if col not in df.columns:
            continue  # skip columns that don't exist
        Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
        Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
        IQR = Q3 - Q1
        upper_bound = Q3 + k * IQR
        filter_col = df[col] <= upper_bound
        filter_total &= filter_col

    filtered_df = df[filter_total]

    return filtered_df
In [5]:

df_eda = train_df.copy()
df_eda.drop(columns=['id', 'host_name', 'host_id'], inplace=True)

In [13]:
# Since there where so many neighbourhoods, we will group the rare ones into "Other" based on a threshold 
# of 1% frequency.
threshold = 0.01  
counts = df_eda_filtered['neighbourhood'].value_counts(normalize=True)
rare_neigh = counts[counts < threshold].index
df_eda_filtered['neighbourhood_grouped'] = df_eda_filtered['neighbourhood'].apply(
    lambda x: 'Other' if x in rare_neigh else x
)
df_eda_filtered = df_eda_filtered.drop(columns=['neighbourhood'])
df_eda_filtered = df_eda_filtered.rename(columns={'neighbourhood_grouped': 'neighbourhood'})


for col in categorical_features:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    sns.boxplot(x=col, y='reviews_per_month', data=df_eda_filtered)
    plt.title(f"Reviews per Month by {col}")
    plt.xticks(rotation=90)
    plt.show()
print(BOLD + "Figure 4: Reviews per Month by Categorical Features" + END + "\n")

Figure 4: Reviews per Month by Categorical Features

4. Feature engineering¶

The code for the feature engineering, the columnTransformer and the pipelines are in the preproccessing and transformation section.

Using the feature last_review we will create two new ones: (1) Days_since_last_review: the number of days since the listing was last reviewed.

  • If last_review is NaN then days_since_last_review will be set to last_review.max().
  • The rational is that having no review is a poor indication of popularity and thus we will treat it as if the last re (2) never_reviewed: a binary feature which will be 1 if last_review is NaN and 0 otherwise.

Here is a summary of how we will treat the data (some of this was addressed in the EDA)

  • Impute NaN's in reviews_per_month to 0.
  • Any category in neighbourhood with a frequecny of less than 1% will be grouped into 'other'
  • Engineer new features from last_review and then drop last_review.

5. Preprocessing and transformations¶

In [14]:
features_with_upper_outliers = [
    'price', 
    'minimum_nights', 
    'number_of_reviews',
    'calculated_host_listings_count'
]

numerical_features = [
    'price',
    'minimum_nights', 
    'number_of_reviews', 
    'calculated_host_listings_count', 
    'availability_365', 
    'latitude', 
    'longitude'
]

numerical_features_to_log = [
    'price', 
    'minimum_nights', 
    'number_of_reviews',
    'calculated_host_listings_count', 
    'availability_365'
]

date_feature = ['last_review']

categorical_features = ['neighbourhood_group', 'neighbourhood', 'room_type']
In [15]:
# Since we are imputing the target variable to be 0, and dropping columns/rows, 
# I will redo the train test split here.

df2 = pd.read_csv(PATH)
# Drop unnecessary features
df2 = df2.drop(columns=['id', 'host_id', 'host_name'])  
# Impute target variable
df2['reviews_per_month'] = df2['reviews_per_month'].fillna(0)   
train_df, test_df = train_test_split(df2, test_size=0.3, random_state=123)

# Remove outliers from training data
train_df = remove_upper_outliers_iqr(train_df, features_with_upper_outliers, k=3, verbose=True) 

X_train = train_df.drop(columns=['reviews_per_month'])
y_train = train_df['reviews_per_month']

X_test = test_df.drop(columns=['reviews_per_month'])
y_test = test_df['reviews_per_month']
In [16]:
# Custom skileartransformer classes  
# n Used chatGPT to help create these custom transformers
# I had the code to do these transformations, but wanted to make them reusable
# Prompt: "From this code [code for the transformation given] create a sklearn transformer class"

class DateTransformer(BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin):
    """Creates 'days_since_last_review' and 'never_reviewed' features."""
    def __init__(self, reference_date=pd.Timestamp('2019-07-08')):
        self.reference_date = reference_date
        
    def fit(self, X, y=None):
        return self

    def transform(self, X):
        X = X.copy()
        last_review = pd.to_datetime(X['last_review'], errors='coerce')
        
        # Feature 1: Days since last review
        days_since = (self.reference_date - last_review).dt.days
        days_since = days_since.fillna(days_since.max())  # Fill NaNs with max (meaning: never reviewed)
        
        # Feature 2: Never reviewed flag
        never_reviewed = last_review.isna().astype(int)
        
        return pd.DataFrame({
            'days_since_last_review': days_since,
            'never_reviewed': never_reviewed
        })
    
class RareCategoryGrouper(BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin):
    """Groups rare categories (< threshold fraction) into 'Other'."""
    def __init__(self, threshold=0.01):
        self.threshold = threshold

    def fit(self, X, y=None):
        # Ensure X is a DataFrame
        X = pd.DataFrame(X)
        # Store common categories for each column
        self.common_ = {}
        for col in X.columns:
            freq = X[col].value_counts(normalize=True)
            self.common_[col] = freq[freq >= self.threshold].index.tolist()
        return self

    def transform(self, X):
        X = pd.DataFrame(X).copy()
        for col in X.columns:
            X[col] = X[col].apply(lambda val: val if val in self.common_[col] else 'Other')
        return X
    
def selective_log_transform(X):
    X = X.copy()
    for col in X.columns:
        if col in numerical_features_to_log:
            X[col] = np.log1p(X[col])  # log(1+x)
    return X
In [45]:
num_pipeline = Pipeline(steps=[
    ('log', FunctionTransformer(selective_log_transform, validate=False, feature_names_out="one-to-one")),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler())
])

cat_pipeline = ColumnTransformer(transformers=[
    ('neigh_group', Pipeline([
        ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'))
    ]), ['neighbourhood_group']),

    ('neigh', Pipeline([
        ('rare_grouper', RareCategoryGrouper(threshold=0.01)),  # use the new class here
        ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'))
    ]), ['neighbourhood']),

    ('room', Pipeline([
        ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore'))
    ]), ['room_type'])
])

# Make new feature from last_review, but only scale days_since_last_review
date_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('date_features', DateTransformer()),
    ('split_features', ColumnTransformer([
        ('scale_days', StandardScaler(), ['days_since_last_review']),
        ('keep_flag', 'passthrough', ['never_reviewed'])
    ]))
])

preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(transformers=[
    ('num', num_pipeline, numerical_features),
    ('cat', cat_pipeline, categorical_features),
    ('date', date_pipeline, date_feature)
])

6. Baseline model¶

In [18]:
dummy_regressor = DummyRegressor(strategy='mean')
dummy_regressor.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_dummy = dummy_regressor.predict(X_test)
In [19]:
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_dummy)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_dummy)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_dummy)

print(BOLD + "Table 3: Baseline DummyRegressor performance with mean strategy:\n" + END)
print(f"  MAE  = {mae:.4f}")
print(f"  RMSE = {rmse:.4f}")
print(f"  R²   = {r2:.4f}")

Table 3: Baseline DummyRegressor performance with mean strategy:

  MAE  = 1.1066
  RMSE = 1.5623
  R²   = -0.0077

The DummyRegressor was trained on the training set and evaluated on the test set. The results show a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.0853, a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 1.5705, and an R² score of -0.0184.

These results indicate that, on average, the baseline predictions are off by about 1.1066 reviews per month, and some listings deviate further from the mean, as reflected by the higher RMSE of 1.5623. The negative R² shows that this naive model performs slightly worse than simply predicting the mean of the test set. This baseline provides a minimum performance standard, and any model using feature engineering and preprocessing should outperform it, achieving lower error and a positive R².

7. Linear models¶

In [20]:
ridge_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', Ridge())
])
scoring = {
    'rmse': 'neg_mean_squared_error',
    'r2': 'r2'
}
param_grid = {'regressor__alpha': [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 500]}
kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

ridge_gridSearch = GridSearchCV(ridge_pipeline, param_grid, cv=kf, 
                                scoring=scoring, refit='rmse', 
                                return_train_score=True,
                                n_jobs=-1)
results = ridge_gridSearch.fit(X_train, y_train)
In [21]:
cv_results_df = pd.DataFrame(ridge_gridSearch.cv_results_)

# Add RMSE (take sqrt of mean_test_score)
cv_results_df['mean_test_rmse'] = np.sqrt(-cv_results_df['mean_test_rmse'])
cv_results_df['std_test_rmse'] = np.sqrt(cv_results_df['std_test_rmse'])

# Select only relevant columns
print(BOLD + "\nTable 4: Ridge Regression Grid Search CV Results for alpha\n" + END)
display_columns = ['param_regressor__alpha', 'mean_test_rmse', 'std_test_rmse', 'mean_test_r2', 'std_test_r2']
cv_results_df[display_columns]


Table 4: Ridge Regression Grid Search CV Results for alpha
Out[21]:
param_regressor__alpha mean_test_rmse std_test_rmse mean_test_r2 std_test_r2
0 0.001 1.075788 0.186307 0.421829 0.012731
1 0.010 1.075786 0.186310 0.421831 0.012729
2 0.100 1.075779 0.186314 0.421838 0.012723
3 0.500 1.075744 0.186367 0.421877 0.012695
4 1.000 1.075708 0.186375 0.421917 0.012652
5 5.000 1.075407 0.186638 0.422248 0.012358
6 10.000 1.075077 0.187012 0.422612 0.012003
7 25.000 1.074315 0.188296 0.423456 0.011079
8 50.000 1.073630 0.190605 0.424226 0.009904
9 75.000 1.073451 0.192878 0.424446 0.009066
10 100.000 1.073591 0.194925 0.424319 0.008466
11 500.000 1.083317 0.208508 0.413948 0.007001

The Ridge regression model was trained with a range of alpha values from 0.001 to 500, performing 5-fold cross-validation for each. The results show that an alpha of 75 produced the best performance, having the lowest mean CV RMSE of 1.0734 and the highest mean R² of 0.4244 (Table 4). The standard deviation for the RMSE was 0.19, indicating relative stability (Table 4). Alpha values higher or lower than 75 increased the RMSE and decreased the R², showing underfitting.

8. Different models¶

In [22]:
rmse_scorer = make_scorer(root_mean_squared_error)
kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
In [23]:
# Model: Random Forest Regressor
rf_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=5, random_state=42))
])

results_rf = []

rf_cv = cross_validate(
    rf_pipeline, X_train, y_train,
    scoring={'rmse': rmse_scorer, 'r2': 'r2'},
    cv=kf,
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)


results_rf.append({
    'Model': 'RandomForest',
    'Mean RMSE': np.mean(rf_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Std RMSE': np.std(rf_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Mean Train RMSE': np.mean(rf_cv['train_rmse']),
    'Mean CV R²': np.mean(rf_cv['test_r2']),
    'Std CV R²': np.std(rf_cv['test_r2']),
    'Fit Time': np.mean(rf_cv['fit_time']),
    'Score Time': np.mean(rf_cv['score_time'])
})
In [24]:
results_rf = pd.DataFrame(results_rf)
print(BOLD + "\nTable 5: Random Forest Regressor CV Results\n" + END)
results_rf

Model Mean RMSE Std RMSE Mean Train RMSE Mean CV R² Std CV R² Fit Time Score Time

# Model 2: Gradient Boosting Regressor
gb_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=20, learning_rate=0.1, random_state=42))
])

results_gb = []

gb_cv = cross_validate(
    gb_pipeline, X_train, y_train,
    scoring={'rmse': rmse_scorer, 'r2': 'r2'},
    cv=kf,
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

results_gb.append({
    'Model': 'GradientBoosting',
    'Mean RMSE': np.mean(gb_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Std RMSE': np.std(gb_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Mean Train RMSE': np.mean(gb_cv['train_rmse']),
    'Mean CV R²': np.mean(gb_cv['test_r2']),
    'Std CV R²': np.std(gb_cv['test_r2']),
    'Fit Time': np.mean(gb_cv['fit_time']),
    'Score Time': np.mean(gb_cv['score_time'])
})

# Model 3: K-Nearest Neighbors Regressor

results_knn = []

knn_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=5))
])

knn_cv = cross_validate(
    knn_pipeline, X_train, y_train,
    scoring={'rmse': rmse_scorer, 'r2': 'r2'},
    cv=kf,
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

results_knn.append({
    'Model': 'KNN',
    'Mean RMSE': np.mean(knn_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Std RMSE': np.std(knn_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Mean Train RMSE': np.mean(knn_cv['train_rmse']),
    'Mean CV R²': np.mean(knn_cv['test_r2']),
    'Std CV R²': np.std(knn_cv['test_r2']),
    'Fit Time': np.mean(knn_cv['fit_time']),
    'Score Time': np.mean(knn_cv['score_time'])
})
In [30]:
results_knn = pd.DataFrame(results_knn)
print(BOLD + "\nTable 7: K-Nearest Neighbors CV Results\n" + END)
results_knn


Table 7: K-Nearest Neighbors CV Results
Out[30]:
Model Mean RMSE Std RMSE Mean Train RMSE Mean CV R² Std CV R² Fit Time Score Time
0 KNN 1.085391 0.017073 0.881811 0.411345 0.012748 0.441588 50.70278
In [28]:
results_svr = []

svr_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', SVR(kernel='rbf', C=1.0, epsilon=0.1))
])

svr_cv = cross_validate(
    svr_pipeline, X_train, y_train,
    scoring={'rmse': rmse_scorer, 'r2': 'r2'},
    cv=kf,
    return_train_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

results_svr.append({
    'Model': 'SVR',
    'Mean RMSE': np.mean(svr_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Std RMSE': np.std(svr_cv['test_rmse']),
    'Mean Train RMSE': np.mean(svr_cv['train_rmse']),
    'Mean CV R²': np.mean(svr_cv['test_r2']),
    'Std CV R²': np.std(svr_cv['test_r2']),
    'Fit Time': np.mean(svr_cv['fit_time']),
    'Score Time': np.mean(svr_cv['score_time'])
})
In [29]:
results_svr = pd.DataFrame(results_svr)
print(BOLD + "\nTable 8: Support Vector Regressor CV Results\n" + END)
results_svr


Table 8: Support Vector Regressor CV Results
Out[29]:
Model Mean RMSE Std RMSE Mean Train RMSE Mean CV R² Std CV R² Fit Time Score Time
0 SVR 0.994893 0.022285 0.971542 0.505532 0.01106 34.123144 5.414588

Cross-validation on the training data showed clear differences in generalization, overfitting, and runtime. Gradient boosting achieved the best performance, with a mean CV RMSE of about 0.905 and mean CV R² of about 0.591, and its mean train RMSE was almost the same as its mean test RMSE (~0.90 vs ~0.90), which suggests little train–test gap and a well-balanced fit rather than severe overfitting.

Random Forest and SVR also have lower RMSE and higher R² than Ridge, but neither outperformed GBR. Random Forest showed overfitting (much lower train RMSE than validation RMSE). SVR had a much longer fit time than GBR.

KNN did not out-perform Ridge in any metric and it also took the longest time to score.

SVR also beat Ridge on both metrics (mean CV RMSE ~0.995, mean CV R² ~0.506), with only a modest train–test RMSE gap (~0.97 vs ~0.99), indicating mild overfitting relative to random forest but at the cost of much longer fit time (~34 seconds per fold on average compared to a few seconds for boosting).

9. Feature selection¶

In [37]:
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

select_gbr = SelectFromModel(
    GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=20, learning_rate=0.1, random_state=42),
    threshold="median"
)

pipe_ridge_model_based = make_pipeline(
    preprocessor,
    select_gbr,
    Ridge(alpha=50)
)

cv_results = cross_validate(
    pipe_ridge_model_based, X_train, y_train,
    scoring={'rmse': rmse_scorer, 'r2': 'r2'},
    return_train_score=True,
    cv=kf,
    n_jobs=-1
)

pd.DataFrame(cv_results).mean()

pipe_ridge_model_based.fit(X_train, y_train)

selector = pipe_ridge_model_based.named_steps["selectfrommodel"]
n_before = selector.estimator_.n_features_in_
n_after = int(selector.get_support().sum())

print(f"Number of features before selection: {n_before}")
print(f"Number of features after selection: {n_after}")

Number of features before selection: 41
Number of features after selection: 41
In [36]:
pd.DataFrame(cv_results).mean()
Out[36]:
fit_time      19.603451
score_time     0.059603
test_rmse      1.102789
train_rmse     1.102639
test_r2        0.392490
train_r2       0.392760
dtype: float64

The GBR cross-validation in the previous question acheived:

Model	           Test RMSE	  Std RMSE   Train RMSE     Mean R²      Std R²	 
GradientBoosting   0.904498	     0.018064    0.899838    0.591341    0.004376

Feature selection using Gradient Boosting did not remove any features, as all features had importance values above the median threshold. Despite this, model performance slightly decreased. This is likely due to the additional model used for feature selection introducing variance and noise into the pipeline, particularly since the Gradient Boosting model was not strongly tuned.



10. Hyperparameter optimization¶

In [44]:
#Gradient Boosting Regressor
import optuna

def objective_gb(trial):
    params = {
        "n_estimators": trial.suggest_int("n_estimators", 50, 300),
        "max_depth":   trial.suggest_int("max_depth", 2, 6),
        "learning_rate": trial.suggest_float("learning_rate", 0.01, 0.2, log=True),
        "min_samples_split": trial.suggest_int("min_samples_split", 2, 12),
        "random_state": 42,
    }
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('preprocess', preprocessor),
        ('regressor', GradientBoostingRegressor(**params))
    ])
    scores = cross_val_score(pipe, X_train, y_train,
                             scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error", cv=kf, n_jobs=-1)
    return -scores.mean()

study_gb = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study_gb.optimize(objective_gb, n_trials=30, timeout=None, show_progress_bar=True)
print("GB best RMSE:", study_gb.best_value, "params:", study_gb.best_params)

In [45]:
# Random Forest
def objective_rf(trial):
    params = {
        "n_estimators": trial.suggest_int("n_estimators", 25, 300),
        "max_depth": 5,
        "random_state": 42,
        "n_jobs": -1
    }
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('preprocess', preprocessor),
        ('regressor', RandomForestRegressor(**params))
    ])
    scores = cross_val_score(pipe, X_train, y_train,
                             scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error", cv=kf, n_jobs=-1)
    return -scores.mean()

study_rf = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study_rf.optimize(objective_rf, n_trials=20, show_progress_bar=True)
print("RF best RMSE:", study_rf.best_value, "params:", study_rf.best_params)

In [46]:
# KNN
def objective_knn(trial):
    params = {"n_neighbors": trial.suggest_int("n_neighbors", 3, 50)}
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('preprocess', preprocessor),
        ('regressor', KNeighborsRegressor(**params))
    ])
    scores = cross_val_score(pipe, X_train, y_train,
                             scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error", cv=kf, n_jobs=-1)
    return -scores.mean()

study_knn = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study_knn.optimize(objective_knn, n_trials=25, show_progress_bar=True)
print("KNN best RMSE:", study_knn.best_value, "params:", study_knn.best_params)

KNN best RMSE: 1.0370105083365868 params: {'n_neighbors': 23}
In [47]:
# SVR
def objective_svr(trial):
    params = {
        "C": trial.suggest_float("C", 0.1, 10.0, log=True),
        "epsilon": 0.1,             
        "kernel": "rbf"
    }
    pipe = Pipeline([
        ('preprocess', preprocessor),
        ('regressor', SVR(**params))
    ])
    scores = cross_val_score(pipe, X_train, y_train,
                             scoring="neg_root_mean_squared_error", cv=kf, n_jobs=-1)
    return -scores.mean()

study_svr = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study_svr.optimize(objective_svr, n_trials=25, show_progress_bar=True)
print("SVR best RMSE:", study_svr.best_value, "params:", study_svr.best_params)

SVR best RMSE: 0.9660318393672813 params: {'C': 9.74193276094455}

For each of model hyper parameter optimization led to an improvement, finding paramaters that performed better than when we trained the model with default params. So there is a performance gain when tuning hyperparameters which is obvious. There was more deviation in scores when multiple hyper parameters where tuned at once as seen in the gradient boosting example, compared to when only looking at one parameter the scores didn't change much. I also had used optuna previously and so I wanted to see each trials result so I went with this version to see what the variances looked like when exploring the different hyperparameters.

The best RMSE was obtained by GBR, with a value of: 0.8469174515275977 and with the hyper-paramaters: {'n_estimators': 243, 'max_depth': 5, 'learning_rate': 0.04086904131236621, 'min_samples_split': 6}

11. Interpretation and feature importances¶

In [ ]:
from itertools import chain

gb_pipeline_tuned = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=243, max_depth=5, learning_rate=0.04086904131236621, min_samples_split=6, random_state=42))
])

gb_pipeline_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)

pre = gb_pipeline_tuned.named_steps["preprocess"]
model = gb_pipeline_tuned.named_steps["regressor"]


cat_ct = pre.named_transformers_['cat']
ohe_names_all = []
for name, pipe, cols in cat_ct.transformers_:
    ohe = pipe.named_steps['onehot']
    ohe_names_all.append(ohe.get_feature_names_out(cols))
cat_names = list(chain.from_iterable(ohe_names_all))

num_names  = numerical_features
date_names = ['days_since_last_review', 'never_reviewed']
feature_names = np.array(num_names + cat_names + date_names)

X_train_transformed = pre.transform(X_train).toarray()
X_test_transformed = pre.transform(X_test).toarray()

X_train_enc = pd.DataFrame(
    data=X_train_transformed,
    columns=feature_names,
    index=X_train.index,
)

X_test_enc = pd.DataFrame(
    data=X_test_transformed,
    columns=feature_names,
    index=X_test.index,
)

shap.initjs()
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
train_shap_values = explainer(X_train_enc)
test_shap_values = explainer(X_test_enc)

<class 'numpy.ndarray'>
(25837, 41)
In [56]:
shap.summary_plot(train_shap_values, X_train_enc)

According to the shap summary plot the features that seemed to matter the most were the top 7 of the plot. For example days_since_last review shows that when the feature values were low (fewer days since last review) were predicting a frequency of more reviews, so places that were recently reviewed are more popular. Number of reviews also played a role where the higher the feature value(more reviews) predict that it will be more popular.

12. Results on the test set¶

In [58]:
gb_pipeline_tuned = Pipeline([
    ('preprocess', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=243, max_depth=5, learning_rate=0.04086904131236621, min_samples_split=6, random_state=42))
])

gb_pipeline_tuned.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_test = gb_pipeline_tuned.predict(X_test)
In [59]:
mse_test = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_test)
rmse_test = np.sqrt(mse_test)
mae_test = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred_test)
r2_test = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_test)

print(f"Test RMSE: {rmse_test:.4f}")
print(f"Test MAE: {mae_test:.4f}")
print(f"Test R²:  {r2_test:.4f}")

Test RMSE: 0.8951
Test MAE: 0.4590
Test R²:  0.6692

The tuned Gradient Boosting Regressor stayed consistent between cross-validation (Optuna objective on training folds) and the held-out test set. Test RMSE (0.895) is modestly higher than the best CV RMSE (~0.847), which is expected: the test set was not used for tuning, and a small gap suggests good generalization rather than severe overfitting.

Test R² (0.669) is higher than the mean CV R² from the initial default GBR run (~0.59). That comparison mixes different settings (default vs tuned model) and a single random train/test split, so a small mismatch is normal. Test R² should be compared to the tuned model's CV performance for a stricter check; in any case, the model explains a substantial share of variance on unseen listings.

Hyperparameter search on the training data introduces some optimization bias (the CV score is slightly optimistic). The workflow still respects a final lockbox test set for an honest estimate of deployment error.

In [60]:
i1, i2 = 1, 10

shap.initjs()
shap.force_plot(explainer.expected_value, test_shap_values[i1].values, X_test_enc.iloc[i1,:])

In [61]:
shap.force_plot(explainer.expected_value, test_shap_values[i2].values, X_test_enc.iloc[i2,:])
Out[61]:
Visualization omitted, Javascript library not loaded!
Have you run `initjs()` in this notebook? If this notebook was from another user you must also trust this notebook (File -> Trust notebook). If you are viewing this notebook on github the Javascript has been stripped for security. If you are using JupyterLab this error is because a JupyterLab extension has not yet been written.

In the first example, the model predicts a higher number of reviews per month (around 2.47) compared to the overall average prediction of 0.96. This is mainly because the feature number_of_reviews strongly pushes the prediction upward, outweighing smaller downward effects from other features such as days_since_last_review and longitude.

In contrast, the second example predicts a much lower value of around 0.02 reviews per month, since most influential features in this case (particularly days_since_last_review and number_of_reviews) push the prediction downward, indicating that the listing has not been reviewed recently and is less active.

13. Summary of results¶

This project built an end-to-end supervised regression pipeline on the 2019 NYC Airbnb Open Data to predict reviews_per_month as a proxy for listing activity. After EDA (distributions, outliers, missingness) and domain-driven cleaning (e.g. treating zero-review listings as 0 reviews/month), we engineered time-based features from last_review (days_since_last_review, never_reviewed), applied log transforms and scaling to skewed numerics, and used one-hot encoding with rare-level grouping for high-cardinality neighbourhood. Everything was wrapped in sklearn Pipeline / ColumnTransformer to avoid leakage.

We evaluated models primarily with RMSE (same units as the target) and R². A dummy mean baseline on the test set showed large error and negative R², confirming the target is learnable. Ridge with grid search (GridSearchCV) improved substantially (CV RMSE ~1.07, R² ~0.42). We then compared Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, KNN, and SVR with 5-fold cross-validation; gradient boosting had the best CV RMSE/R² and the healthiest train vs validation gap, while random forest showed stronger overfitting and KNN did not beat Ridge with the chosen settings.

Hyperparameter tuning used Optuna (Bayesian-style search) on the training folds; GBR achieved the best CV RMSE (~0.847) among the tuned models. The final tuned GBR was refit on all training data and assessed once on the 30% holdout test set: RMSE 0.895, MAE 0.459, R² 0.669—clearly outperforming the linear baseline and dummy model.

SHAP (TreeExplainer) indicated that predictions lean heavily on review history—especially number_of_reviews and days_since_last_review—which aligns with the problem: popularity is observed through past engagement. Many location and listing-detail features contribute less at the margin, which is a useful sanity check but also highlights limits of the target (reviews/month is only a rough proxy for "popularity").

Limitations and possible improvements¶

  • Optimization bias: Tune with nested CV or a dedicated validation split if you need an unbiased estimate of CV error; keep one final test set untouched.
  • Target definition: Explore alternatives (e.g. log-target, clipping extremes) or complementary targets; the current label mixes activity and listing age.
  • Text and identity: Listing name was dropped; TF–IDF or embeddings on titles, or careful host-level aggregates, could add signal without leaking if designed properly.
  • Stronger learners / ensembling: Histogram-based boosting (e.g. HistGradientBoostingRegressor, XGBoost/LightGBM) often improves tabular regression with less tuning cost.
  • Spatial/temporal validation: If the use case is forecasting, consider grouped splits (e.g. by neighbourhood or time) to measure true generalization.
  • Uncertainty: Add prediction intervals (e.g. quantile regression or conformal methods) for business decisions.

Overall, the workflow demonstrates reproducible preprocessing, proper validation, nonlinear modeling, systematic hyperparameter search, and model interpretation—with a final model that generalizes well on held-out data relative to linear and baseline competitors.
